critique.summary: The article details the development journey of implementing multiplayer functionality in the game Teardown, highlighting the technical challenges of synchronizing a dynamic, destructible voxel world over a network. It describes the hybrid deterministic and state synchronization approach, scripting adaptations, UI handling, and the complex merging process with the main game branch.
mainPoints: Multiplayer was highly requested but initially considered unrealistic due to the complexity of synchronizing physics and voxel destruction.
An early naive synchronization experiment in 2021 failed due to bandwidth issues, but a community mod (TDMP) demonstrated rudimentary multiplayer feasibility.
The final approach uses a hybrid system: deterministic commands for destruction and reliable data, and unreliable state synchronization for object transforms and player positions.
Scripts were adapted to run partly on server and client with shared state and remote calls to support modding and multiplayer.
Terminal UI rendering was handled by streaming draw command deltas from server to clients, inspired by the X Window System.
Initially planned as a separate game, multiplayer was merged into the main Teardown codebase, a complex and lengthy process involving weekly merges and backend changes.
Backwards compatibility with existing mods was prioritized, though multiplayer disables scripts in old mods.
Late join-in-progress was solved by sending a deterministic command stream to new clients, with limits on buffer size to manage performance.
Development tools evolved from running multiple instances in one window to separate processes due to merge complexities.
The author expresses pride in the achievement despite the implementation being complex and inelegant.
critique.truthfulness: The article appears to be a firsthand account from a developer involved in the multiplayer implementation of Teardown, providing detailed technical explanations consistent with known challenges of multiplayer synchronization in physics-based voxel games. The description of the hybrid deterministic and state synchronization approach aligns with established multiplayer networking techniques. The mention of the community mod TDMP is verifiable and known within the Teardown community. The technical details about floating point determinism, command streaming, and UI handling are plausible and consistent with industry practices. There are no obvious factual inaccuracies, and the article does not make unsupported claims beyond the author's experience and perspective.
considerations: The article does not provide external sources or perspectives beyond the author's account, which limits independent verification.
It does not discuss potential player experience issues such as latency effects or desynchronization in detail.
The impact of the multiplayer implementation on game performance or player base reception is not covered.
Security considerations related to hosting peer-to-peer sessions are not mentioned.
The article focuses on technical implementation and does not address business or community management aspects of multiplayer deployment.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the technical challenges and achievements of implementing multiplayer in Teardown, with particular focus on deterministic physics and data synchronization. Commenters express admiration for the engineering effort, raise questions about physics implementation, and discuss data transfer methods for multiplayer synchronization.

comments.author: jmgao
comments.text:
> For the longest time (and for good reasons), floating point operations were considered unsafe for deterministic purposes. That is still true to some extent, but the picture is more nuanced than that. I have since learned a lot about floating point determinism, and these days I know it is mostly safe if you know how to navigate around the pitfalls.

If you're only concerned about identical binaries on x86, it's not too bad because AMD and Intel tend to have intentionally identical implementations of most floating point operations, with the exception of a few of the approximate reciprocal SSE instructions (rcpps, rsqrtps, etc). Modern x86 instructions tend to have their exact results strictly defined to avoid this kind of inconsistency: https://software.intel.com/en-us/articles/reference-implemen...

If you want this to work across ARM and x86 (or even multiple ARM vendors), you are screwed, and need to restrict yourself to using only the basic arithmetic operations and reimplement everything else yourself.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced and technically accurate explanation of floating point determinism relevant to multiplayer physics, supported by credible references.

comments.author: Sweepi
comments.text:
"1.Serialize the entire scene, compress the data, and pass it to the joining client. We already do full scene serialization for quicksave and quickload, so this is possible, but the files are large: 30-50 MB is common, often more, so transfer would take a while.

[...]

3. Record the deterministic command stream, pass it to the joining client, and have that client apply all changes to the loaded scene before joining the game. The amount of data is much smaller than in option 2 since we’re not sending any voxel data, but applying the changes can take a while since it involves a lot computation.

Once we started investigating option 3 we realized it was actually less data than we anticipated, but we still limit the buffer size and disable join-in-progress when it fills up. This allows late joins up to a certain amount of scene changes, beyond which applying the commands would simply take an unreasonably long time. "

So [1] is not an option for players who want to do it that way?
comments.analysis: The comment offers a detailed and plausible explanation of multiplayer synchronization strategies, reflecting an understanding of the technical challenges.

comments.author: HexDecOctBin
comments.text:
If someone from Teardown dev team is here, did you guys ever tried to do the physics in voxel space? If I understand correctly, Teardown convert each physics chunk into a mesh and feeds all the meshes into a traditional physics engine. But this means that the voxel models remain constrained to the voxel grid only locally but not globally.

I have been trying to figure out a way to do physics completely in voxel space to ensure a global grid. But I have not been able to find any theory of Newtonian Mechanics that would work in discretised space (Movable Cellular Automata was the closest). I wonder if anyone in the Teardown dev team tried to solve this problem?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid and insightful technical question about the physics implementation, showing understanding of voxel-based simulations.

comments.author: kajkojednojajko
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing Teardown when it first came out. It was already a technological marvel back then, so it's even more impressive that they managed to make the simulation deterministic to add multiplayer several years after release. Clearly top tier engineers.

Cool to see that the game is owned by Coffee Stain now, too. Satisfactory has been handled well by them, so I'm optimistic about the future of Teardown as well.
comments.analysis: While mostly opinion, the comment accurately reflects the known timeline and ownership, providing a positive perspective.

comments.author: Aeolun
comments.text:
I really love Teardown, and I’m absolutely baffled at the bizarre performance that game manages to push on even very large worlds.

I’ve tried to load teardown levels in a homegrown engine and I always end up stuttering like hell as soon as GI becomes involved (or even before that).

I’m going to finally manage to replicate it, and then the new engine will be released and raise the bar again xD
comments.analysis: An experiential comment that highlights the game's technical impressiveness but is subjective and anecdotal.

comments.author: bjord
comments.text: I highly respect the implicit decision to forgo repeat purchases by merging into the original game, considering how much work was clearly involved. I haven't played it, but I hope for sustainability's sake, there are sufficient (purely cosmetic) microtransactions to cover their development costs.
comments.analysis: A reasonable opinion on business strategy with no technical claims, reflecting common industry practices.

comments.author: hannasanarion
comments.text:
I feel like there's detail missing in the blog.

The "Reliable vs Unreliable" section implies that different parts of the scene are sent using a strict-ordering protocol so that the transforms happen in the same order on every client, but other parts happen in a state update stream with per client queueing.

But which is which? Which events are sent TCP and which are UDP (and is that literally what they're doing, or only a metaphor?)

Really the economy of the text in the blog seems backwards, this section has one short paragraph explaining the concept of deterministic event ordering as important for keeping things straight, and then 3 paragraphs about how player position and velocity are synced in the same way as any other game. I want read more about the part that makes teardown unique!
comments.analysis: A constructive critique pointing out missing technical details, showing engagement but no factual claims.

comments.author: DimitriBouriez
comments.text: Wouldn't it have been simpler (even if technically heavy) to host the game on a single machine and just stream each player's camera? That way all the physics would be computed in real time on one computer, and each player would just receive a different video stream.
comments.analysis: A plausible alternative approach but ignores latency and interactivity issues inherent to streaming solutions.

comments.author: tietjens
comments.text: In my opinion one of the most impressive independent games published on Steam in the last years.
comments.analysis: A subjective praise comment without technical or factual content.

comments.author: tantalor
comments.text:
For a bit more context, here's the announcement video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XfcCyMQ13XM

comments.analysis: Provides a useful resource but no analysis or original content.

comments.author: xecaz
comments.text: Looking forward to play the MP version with my son who also loves the game. I worry that the game will get heavier and maybe no longer work of the steamdeck, will it?
comments.analysis: Expresses personal concern without technical insight or factual information.

comments.author: asadm
comments.text: do they have split screen multiplayer? I wish they did.
comments.analysis: A simple question that does not contribute to the technical discussion.

comments.author: esperent
comments.text:
Looks like someone needs a better web host.

> Due to protection of web servers from repeated attacks, we were forced to restrict access to administrative interface of web pages to selected countries. If you are currently in a foreign country, please sign in to WebAdmin, proceed to your domain management and disable this GeoIP filter in OneClick Installer section.
comments.analysis: Off-topic comment unrelated to the main discussion about Teardown multiplayer.

comments.author: dopesoap
comments.text:
If only they hadn't stolen the idea at revision and then not paid the person they stole it from.

Douche bags.
comments.analysis: An unsubstantiated and hostile accusation lacking evidence, reducing its credibility.
